Today, I discuss *one* of the many ways to implement a GraphRAG. My current approach uses AWS Neptune Analytics \- “a *memory-optimized* graph database engine for analytics”\[[1](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/neptune-analytics/latest/userguide/what-is-neptune-analytics.html)\]  
Additionally \- and crucially, it can *also* serve as a vector data store, making it a single-shot solution for storing a GraphRAG and a VectorRAG\[[2](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/build-graphrag-applications-using-amazon-bedrock-knowledge-bases/)\].  
The graph data loaded into Neptune Analytics *can* be queried/subject to Exploratory Data Analysis using openCypher queries\[[3](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/neptune/latest/userguide/access-graph-opencypher.html)\] running in a Jupyter Notebook. This is made possible by setting up the Jupyter Notebook in AWS Sagemaker to interact with the graph data. AWS Neptune Analytics also leverages an open-source graph-explorer\[[4](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/neptune/latest/userguide/visualization-graph-explorer.html)\] that allows for visualization of the underlying graph that powers the GraphRAG.  
While it took a couple of iterations to get the workflow right, here is what finally worked for me:

**1\. Setting up the networking aspect correctly:**  
I created a VPC with 4 subnets (2 public and 2 private). I also had a NAT gateway and an Internet Gateway available.  
I created the Neptune Analytics instance within this VPC (inside one of the private subnets) and then created a Jupyter notebook (accessible via AWS Sagemaker) *within the same VPC* as the Neptune Analytics instance. (I actually created it within the same private subnet).  
Next, I created security groups (one for AWS Sagemaker and one for the Neptune Analytics instance) that would allow them to communicate. I also created a security group for my laptop’s IP to allow the browser running the graph-explorer instance to retrieve data from the Neptune Analytics instance over port 443\.

**2.Setting up the KnowledgeBase in AWS Bedrock to use the *existing* Neptune Analytics instance:**  
I uploaded 2 documents \[[Amazon-2024-Annual-Report](https://s2.q4cdn.com/299287126/files/doc_financials/2025/ar/Amazon-2024-Annual-Report.pdf) and [Amazon-2025-Proxy-Statement.pdf](https://s2.q4cdn.com/299287126/files/doc_financials/2025/ar/Amazon-2025-Proxy-Statement.pdf)\] to an S3 bucket and used this bucket as the data source for the Knowledge Base. Titan Embeddings V2 was the model chosen to generate the vector (and graph) embeddings and AWS Nova Pro was chosen to cover the human interaction piece in this RAG approach.  
 I found that adding the documents one-by-one seemed to make the ingestion process go without hiccups.  
By default, AWS Bedrock creates the Neptune Analytics instance in the default VPC and since my networking piece was set up differently, I set it up to instead use the one that I had just created.

**3\. Validating the existence of the graph, using the Jupyter Notebook:**  
The [Neptune graph-notebook magic %%oc](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/neptune/latest/userguide/notebooks-magics.html#notebooks-cell-magics-opencypher) is a great asset to run openCypher queries on the underlying graph. Below are screenshots of some of the openCypher queries:  
![][image1]

![][image2]

![][image3]  
Note: The results of the openCypher queries (RHS) were exported as csv files before taking the screenshots

**4\. Manually updating the proxy URL for the graph explorer:**  
By default the graph explorer has port 8182 appended to the proxy URL as shown below.   
![][image4]  
But since it is interacting with a Neptune Analytics instance, that port number does not apply and getting rid of it allowed the graph to be displayed.  
Note: Cloudwatch logs are invaluable in figuring out the underlying issues. The graph-explorer merely provides a spinner with a message that just says ‘Synchronizing’ and it is hard to gauge how long this process needs to run till a graph shows up.

When the graph does come up, it initially shows up *without* the relationship connectors. And all the nodes ( document, chunk, entity) are rendered in the same color. However, the UI of the graph-explorer is pretty intuitive and the above can quickly be remedied:  
![][image5]

As shown below, it is also possible to tweak the layout of the graph  
![][image6]

![][image7]

![][image8]

5\. Cleanup is (ALWAYS) super-important:  
The Neptune Analytics instance starts accruing charges pretty quickly, but the underlying graph cannot be deleted till the private endpoints that it supports are first deleted.  
**![][image9]**

**My Takeaways:**  
AWS Bedrock and AWS Neptune Analytics make it pretty simple to not only create a GraphRAG but also to visualize and interact with it. However, the underlying networking piece needs to be set up correctly first before *any* of this can proceed successfully.

**References**

[Neptune Analytics](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/neptune-analytics/latest/userguide/what-is-neptune-analytics.html)

[Build GraphRAG applications using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases | Artificial Intelligence](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/build-graphrag-applications-using-amazon-bedrock-knowledge-bases/)

[Accessing the Neptune Graph with openCypher](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/neptune/latest/userguide/access-graph-opencypher.html)

[The open-source graph-explorer \- Amazon Neptune](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/neptune/latest/userguide/visualization-graph-explorer.html)
